title: Where Did the Monk‚Äôs Haircut Come From? A Look at the Rich and Contentious History of the Tonsure
description:
in History, Religion |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

One might assume from a mod¬≠ern view¬≠point that the hair¬≠styles worn by monks a
body:
in History, Religion |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

One might assume from a mod¬≠ern view¬≠point that the hair¬≠styles worn by monks arose to deal with male pat¬≠tern bald¬≠ness anx¬≠i¬≠ety. As in the school uni¬≠form approach, you can‚Äôt sin¬≠gle out one person‚Äôs bald¬≠ness when every¬≠one is bald. But this, again, would be a mod¬≠ern view, full of the van¬≠i¬≠ty the tonsured‚ÄĒthose with reli¬≠gious¬≠ly shaven heads‚ÄĒostensibly vowed to renounce. Accord¬≠ing to the Catholic Ency¬≠clo¬≠pe¬≠dia, the ton¬≠sure (from the Latin verb for ‚Äúto shear‚ÄĚ) began as a ‚Äúbadge of slav¬≠ery‚ÄĚ among Greeks and Romans. It was adopt¬≠ed ‚Äúon this very account‚ÄĚ by ear¬≠ly monas¬≠tic orders, to mark the total sur¬≠ren¬≠der of the will.
Would it sur¬≠prise you, then, to learn that there were ton¬≠sure wars? Prob¬≠a¬≠bly not if you know any¬≠thing about church his¬≠to¬≠ry. Every arti¬≠cle of cloth¬≠ing and of faith has sparked some major con¬≠tro¬≠ver¬≠sy at one time or anoth¬≠er. So too with the ton¬≠sure, of which‚ÄĒwe learn in the Vox video above‚ÄĒthere were three styles. The first, the coro¬≠nal (or Roman or Petrine) ton¬≠sure, is the one we see in count¬≠less Medieval and Renais¬≠sance paint¬≠ings: a bald pate at the crown sur¬≠round¬≠ed by a fringe of hair, pos¬≠si¬≠bly meant to evoke the crown of thorns. Next is the Pauline, a ful¬≠ly shaved head, seen much less in West¬≠ern art since it was ‚Äúused more com¬≠mon¬≠ly in East¬≠ern Ortho¬≠doxy.‚ÄĚ
The third style of ton¬≠sure caused all the trou¬≠ble. Or rather, it was this style that served as a vis¬≠i¬≠ble sign of reli¬≠gious dif¬≠fer¬≠ences between the Roman Catholic Church and the church¬≠es in Britain and Ire¬≠land. ‚ÄúCeltic Catholi¬≠cism was ‚Äėout of sync‚Äô with the Roman Catholic Church,‚ÄĚ notes Vox. ‚ÄúRoman Catholics would use the dif¬≠fer¬≠ences between them to por¬≠tray Celtic Catholi¬≠cism as pagan, or even as an off¬≠shoot, cel¬≠e¬≠brat¬≠ing the pow¬≠er-hun¬≠gry magi¬≠cian, Simon Magus.‚ÄĚ The Celtic ton¬≠sure fell under a cloud, but how exact¬≠ly did it dif¬≠fer from the oth¬≠ers? Since it dis¬≠ap¬≠peared in the ear¬≠ly Mid¬≠dle Ages and few images seem to have sur¬≠vived, no one seems sure.
Daniel McCarthy, fel¬≠low emer¬≠i¬≠tus at Trin¬≠i¬≠ty Col¬≠lege, Dublin set out to solve the mys¬≠tery. He spec¬≠u¬≠lates that the Celtic ton¬≠sure, as you‚Äôll see on a com¬≠put¬≠er-sim¬≠u¬≠lat¬≠ed monk‚Äôs head, was a tri¬≠an¬≠gu¬≠lar shape, with the apex at the front. When the Roman Catholics took over Ire¬≠land, all of the vest¬≠ments, dates, and hair¬≠cuts were slow¬≠ly brought into line with the dom¬≠i¬≠nant view. The prac¬≠tice of ton¬≠sure offi¬≠cial¬≠ly end¬≠ed in 1972, and fell out of favor in Eng¬≠lish-speak¬≠ing coun¬≠tries cen¬≠turies ear¬≠li¬≠er, accord¬≠ing to the Catholic Ency¬≠clo¬≠pe¬≠dia. But in any case, McCarthy sees the ton¬≠sure not as a spurn¬≠ing of fash¬≠ion, but as a cult-like devo¬≠tion to style. In that sense, we can see peo¬≠ple who adopt sim¬≠i¬≠lar hair¬≠cuts around the world as still visu¬≠al¬≠ly sig¬≠nal¬≠ing their mem¬≠ber¬≠ship in some kind of order, reli¬≠gious or oth¬≠er¬≠wise.
Note: An ear¬≠li¬≠er ver¬≠sion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
How a Bal¬≠ti¬≠more Hair¬≠dress¬≠er Became a World-Renowned ‚ÄúHair Archae¬≠ol¬≠o¬≠gist‚ÄĚ of Ancient Rome
Ani¬≠mat¬≠ed: Stephen Fry & Ann Wid¬≠de¬≠combe Debate the Catholic Church
50 Years of Chang¬≠ing David Bowie Hair Styles in One Ani¬≠mat¬≠ed GIF
Josh Jones is a writer and musi¬≠cian based in Durham, NC.

title: The World‚Äôs Greatest Song That Simply Shouldn‚Äôt Exist: The Making of The Beatles‚Äô ‚ÄúA Day in the Life‚ÄĚ
description:
in History, Music |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Were we to take bets on the sin¬≠gle most wide¬≠ly heard alarm clock, the one includ
body:
in History, Music |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Were we to take bets on the sin¬≠gle most wide¬≠ly heard alarm clock, the one includ¬≠ed on the Bea¬≠t¬≠les‚Äô ‚ÄúA Day in the Life‚ÄĚ would sure¬≠ly be a pop¬≠u¬≠lar choice. It comes, you‚Äôll recall, at the begin¬≠ning of the song‚Äôs mid¬≠dle sec¬≠tion, when Paul takes over from John and sings about wak¬≠ing up, comb¬≠ing his hair, catch¬≠ing a bus, and hav¬≠ing a smoke. Giv¬≠en the lyrics‚Äô sub¬≠ject mat¬≠ter, its inclu¬≠sion seems nat¬≠ur¬≠al, and even like one of the most straight¬≠for¬≠ward¬≠ly explic¬≠a¬≠ble choic¬≠es in the entire record¬≠ing. In fact, the alarm clock was nev¬≠er meant to stay in the mix: the band‚Äôs assis¬≠tant Mal Evans set it off as a gag after doing a place¬≠hold¬≠er count of 24 mea¬≠sures in the mid¬≠dle of the ses¬≠sions, but it just hap¬≠pened to work per¬≠fect¬≠ly with the form the song even¬≠tu¬≠al¬≠ly took.
That‚Äôs just one of the for¬≠tu¬≠itous acci¬≠dents music YouTu¬≠ber David Hart¬≠ley recounts in the video above, on the com¬≠plete mak¬≠ing of ‚ÄúA Day in the Life.‚ÄĚ As most Bea¬≠t¬≠les fans know, John real¬≠ly did take his ini¬≠tial inspi¬≠ra¬≠tion from the news¬≠pa¬≠per, and in par¬≠tic¬≠u¬≠lar a cou¬≠ple of arti¬≠cles about a Guin¬≠ness heir‚Äôs fatal car crash and 4,000 holes in Black¬≠burn, Lan¬≠cashire.
But after he wrote his vers¬≠es, he found him¬≠self at a loss as to how to devel¬≠op the song from there. Paul offered the bit he‚Äôd already writ¬≠ten about falling out of bed and drag¬≠ging a comb across his head. It was¬≠n‚Äôt long before the Bea¬≠t¬≠les began record¬≠ing ‚ÄĒ and doing so for the first time, Hart¬≠ley notes, before hav¬≠ing fin¬≠ished writ¬≠ing the song. They had dis¬≠parate pieces of music to join togeth¬≠er, but no idea, yet, of how they‚Äôd do it.
Hence those 24 emp¬≠ty bars. Play¬≠ing a series of ear¬≠ly takes, Hart¬≠ley shows how they got to be filled in, draw¬≠ing heav¬≠i¬≠ly on Here, There, and Every¬≠where: My Life Record¬≠ing the Music of the Bea¬≠t¬≠les, the mem¬≠oir of engi¬≠neer Geoff Emer¬≠ick. John‚Äôs idea of ‚Äúa sound that began real¬≠ly small, and then grad¬≠u¬≠al¬≠ly expand¬≠ed into some¬≠thing huge‚ÄĚ inspired Paul‚Äôs sug¬≠ges¬≠tion of a sym¬≠pho¬≠ny orches¬≠tra, in response to which pro¬≠duc¬≠er George Mar¬≠tin expressed con¬≠cerns about the cost of hir¬≠ing one. Ringo, in turn, jok¬≠ing¬≠ly pro¬≠posed hir¬≠ing half the orches¬≠tra and hav¬≠ing them play the part twice, which turned out to be a prag¬≠mat¬≠ic solu¬≠tion. As for how to end a song like this, the use of ‚Äúmaybe the most famous sin¬≠gle chord in record¬≠ed his¬≠to¬≠ry‚ÄĚ ‚ÄĒ fol¬≠lowed by ‚Äúa high-fre¬≠quen¬≠cy tone only dogs could hear and a short loop of non¬≠sense sound‚ÄĚ ‚ÄĒ¬†came as the result of a process we can only call Beat¬≠lesque.
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
How John Lennon Wrote the Bea¬≠t¬≠les‚Äô Best Song, ‚ÄúA Day in the Life‚ÄĚ
Based in Seoul,¬†Col¬≠in¬†Marshall¬†writes and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬†Books on Cities¬†as well as the books¬†ŪēúÍĶ≠ žöĒžēĹ ÍłąžßÄ¬†(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬†Kore¬≠an Newtro.¬†Fol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬†@colinmarshall.

title: A Stolen Dove Takes Flight Again
description:
Daily Newsletter
                A participatory artwork honors Alex Odeh, the cheerfully sinister worlds of Trevor Winkfield, a
body:
Daily Newsletter
                A participatory artwork honors Alex Odeh, the cheerfully sinister worlds of Trevor Winkfield, and why is Zendaya wearing 3,000-year-old Iranian artifacts?
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Outside a public library in Santa Ana, California, sat a bronze statue of a man with a book under one arm, a dove taking flight from the other. Palestinian-American poet, scholar, and civil rights leader Alex Odeh was assassinated in 1985; nine years later, Algerian-American artist Khalil Bendib made the statue in his honor. It was vandalized with red paint in 1996 and then again the year after; in 2020, the dove ‚ÄĒ¬†a symbol of peace ‚ÄĒ¬†was stolen, before eventually being recovered.¬†Now, artist Jon Rubin is setting that dove free, sending it on a world tour as a living, participatory artwork that brings renewed attention to Odeh‚Äôs life and legacy, a continued history of anti-Arab violence, and efforts to end occupation and genocide in Palestine ‚ÄĒ a quiet prayer for peace. This past weekend, it flew to Los Angeles for an evening of music, poetry, and food; next, it‚Äôll head to the Bay Area, Pittsburgh, and hopefully, to Jifna, Odeh‚Äôs hometown, before returning home to roost.¬†It‚Äôs a timely reminder that art is something meant to be shared in community ‚ÄĒ¬†as Patrick Guetta, the artist featured in this week‚Äôs edition of A View From the Easel, puts it, ‚ÄúOne of my favorite museums is the street.‚ÄĚ Also today, inside the cheerfully sinister worlds of Trevor Winkfield, and Zendaya wearing 3,000-year-old Iranian artifacts to a movie premiere points to bigger problems than out-of-touch celebrities. ‚ÄĒLisa Yin Zhang, associate editorCORRECTION: Our Daily Newsletter from Friday, July 17, mistakenly stated that three antiquities the Manhattan District Attorney‚Äôs Office returned to Mexico were previously held at the Metropolitan Museum of Art, when in fact only one of them was. We regret the error.


                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Artists Announced for Louisiana Contemporary 2026Louisiana Contemporary, presented by The Helis Foundation, celebrates the vitality of artistic practice across Louisiana. Juror Sarah Jones of The Metropolitan Museum of Art selected 50 works by 45 artists for the 2026 exhibition, on view August 1, 2026 - February 14, 2027.
                        
                        
                            Explore this year's artists
                        
                    
            NewsDmitriy Popov was handed a¬†20-year sentence¬†over the fatal stabbing of Black gay professional dancer O‚ÄôShae Sibley. The sentencing comes after Popov was¬†convicted of manslaughter¬†as a hate crime in June.From Our Critics
Opinion

title: Leaks Spotted in New Museum Gallery with WangShui Work as NYC Braces for Floods
description: Flash flooding in New York City this weekend has presented a new test for the New Museum, which experienced a significant water leak on Saturday in
body:
Flash flooding in New York City this weekend has presented a new test for the New Museum, which experienced a significant water leak on Saturday in a gallery housing work by WangShui. Video footage shared with ARTnews and confirmed by the museum as accurate shows water pooling on the gallery floor as staff members move quickly to place buckets beneath active leaks, as well as water streaming down the didactic for ‚ÄúNew Humans: Memories of the Future‚ÄĚ.

	The incident comes months after the opening of the museum‚Äôs $82 million expansion, which faced scrutiny after its debut over reported faulty construction and facilities, including unfinished details and concerns raised by contractors and observers about the building‚Äôs final stages. The museum closed early on Saturday and will remain closed Sunday due to the flash flood warning, according to a statement posted on Instagram.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	A spokesperson for the New Museum told ARTnews via email statement: ‚ÄúAs soon as this leak was discovered, immediate steps were taken to address it and¬†no artwork was harmed. Out of an abundance of caution, the Museum will remain closed tomorrow, Sunday 7/19, and all ticket holders for tomorrow have been refunded.‚ÄĚ

	WangShui‚Äôs oppose the serpent (2024), on view at the New Museum as part of ‚ÄúNew Humans,‚ÄĚ is a monumental entry from a series created for the 2024 Venice Biennale. Composed of hand-anodized aluminum panels colored with cochineal‚ÄĒa natural dye traditionally derived from insects‚ÄĒthe work features intricately etched imagery inspired by marine life, smoke, and earthworms. ARTnews has asked WangShui‚Äôs gallery representation, kurimanzutto, for comment.

	The New Museum‚Äôs $82 million expansion, designed by OMA partners Rem Koolhaas and Shohei Shigematsu, opened to the public in March after high anticipation‚ÄĒand several postponements. While the building initially drew praise for its architecture and ambitious curatorial direction, a subsequent Curbed investigation introduced a more complicated picture of the project. Visitors reported visible signs of unfinished construction, including packing tape on stair railings and paint splatters beneath glass panels. In its reporting, Curbed attributed many of those conditions to what contractors described as a compressed timeline leading up to the museum‚Äôs opening.
	

	Multiple subcontractors quoted in the story said that general contractor Sciame Construction was overseeing several high-profile cultural projects simultaneously‚ÄĒincluding work on the Frick Collection and the Studio Museum in Harlem‚ÄĒwhich they said left the New Museum understaffed. One subcontractor claimed the company had ‚Äúput the B-team on the New Museum,‚ÄĚ while others alleged a frantic final phase of construction, with walls being removed and rebuilt while construction continued near finished areas.

	Sciame disputed those characterizations, telling Curbed that it routinely manages multiple projects concurrently and that the importance of the New Museum expansion ‚Äúwas never in question.‚ÄĚ Sciame added that some construction continued after the opening, which it described as typical for a project of this size, and that the issues observed during previews had since been resolved.

	Another subcontractor involved in the project echoed Sciame‚Äôs assessment, telling Curbed that most large-scale construction projects involve a rush toward completion. An architect unaffiliated with the project similarly characterized the reported issues as largely cosmetic rather than structural, noting that more serious concerns would include inadequate accessibility or ‚Äúleaks in the roof or envelope.‚ÄĚ

title: Armory Show Names Nearly 230 Galleries for 2026 Edition, Pushed to Late September
description: When the Armory Show, one of New York‚Äôs top fairs, relocated to the Javits Center in 2021, it also changed its date, moving from spring to the first
body:
When the Armory Show, one of New York‚Äôs top fairs, relocated to the Javits Center in 2021, it also changed its date, moving from spring to the first week after Labor Day, which the fair touted at the time as a way to ring in the new season. Now, for its 2026 edition, the Armory Show has shifted back a few weeks to run September 24‚Äď27.

	The change in dates will help alleviate the conflict between two important September fairs, which have both been owned by the same company since 2023, when Frieze acquired the Armory Show. Frieze Seoul will remain in the first week of the month (September 2‚Äď5 for this year). The date change, however, also means that there is now less breathing room until Frieze‚Äôs next fair, its original iteration in London.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	‚ÄúFor Frieze, The Armory Show has a particular role: it sits at the heart of New York‚Äôs fall season and gives audiences access to an extraordinary range of galleries from across the Americas and around the world,‚ÄĚ Kristell Chad√©, Frieze‚Äôs executive director of fairs, said in a statement. ‚ÄúThe 2026 edition combines the breadth of the fair with a strong curatorial perspective, strengthening The Armory Show‚Äôs place as a vital moment for galleries, collectors and institutions in New York.‚ÄĚ

	For the upcoming Armory Show, the fair will have around 230 galleries, or roughly the same figures it had in 2025. This edition will also slightly rejigger its floor plan, with the south entrance to the fair focusing on international galleries, while the north entrance highlighting US-based enterprises, with a large contingent of New York‚Äďbased dealerships spread throughout.

	Among the galleries that are set to participate are Victoria Miro, Templon, Mennour, Nara Roesler, Alison Jacques, Perrotin, Michael Rosenfeld Gallery, 303 Gallery, Sean Kelly, Hales, Southern Guild, Vielmetter Gallery, Secrist | Beach, Garth Greenan Gallery, K Contemporary, James Cope Gallery, Yossi Milo, and the newly rebranded Norr Cohan.

	In addition to the main Galleries section, the fair will also feature two sections organized by outside curators: Platform and Focus. Platform, which is for large-scale works, will be by Jovanna Venegas, curator at SculptureCenter in New York; Focus, for solo and two-artist presentations, will be done by Mar√≠a Elena Ortiz, curator at the Modern Art Museum of Fort Worth in Texas. Both sections will focus on highlighting artists with ties to the Caribbean.
	

	Focusing on ‚Äútechnological forms emerging across the Caribbean,‚ÄĚ per a release, projects that will feature in Platform include Bony Ramirez (brought to the fair by New York gallery regularnormal), Jesus Hilario Reyes (Company), Curtis Talwst Santiago (Uffner & Liu), Lucia Hierro (Marc Straus Gallery), Zenobia Lee (Sea View), Margarita Cabrera (Jane Lombard Gallery), Mar√≠a Magdalena Campos-Pons (Barbara Thumm), and Hew Locke (Cristea Roberts Gallery).

	Focus will include booths for Alba Triana (Casa Hoffmann), Gio Swaby and Kachelle Knowles (TERN Gallery), Sof√≠a Del Mar Collins (Chozick Family Art Gallery), Ren√©e Cox (Ethan Cohen Gallery), Simon Benjamin (PATRON), Lizania Cruz (ProxyCo), and Elliot and Erick Jim√©nez (Spinello Projects).

	The fair‚Äôs other two sections have also been expanded, with Presents (for young galleries) now numbering 55 galleries and Solo (single-artist presentations) numbering 25 galleries.

	Presents will feature several first-time exhibitors, including Catinca Tabacaru, Circle Art Gallery, IRL Gallery, Lobster Club, Management, Rajiv Menon, Red Arrow, and The Fridge. They join returning exhibitors like 56 Henry, Alexander Berggruen, Hannah Traore Gallery, Hesse Flatow, JDJ, Kendra Jayne Patrick, k√≥, La Loma, Martha‚Äôs, Rebecca Camacho, and Margot Samel, the winner of the fair‚Äôs 2026 Gramercy International Prize.
	

	The Solo section includes Asya Geisberg Gallery, Halsey McKay Gallery, parrasch heijnen, and What If The World. Additionally, nonprofit spaces will show at the fair, including Robert Blackburn Printmaking Workshop, Brandywine Workshop and Archives, Tierra del Sol Gallery, and El Museo del Barrio, which is the recipient of the Armory Spotlight booth.

	‚ÄúThe Armory Show has always reflected New York‚Äôs openness: to artists, galleries and ideas from across the Americas and the wider world,‚ÄĚ Armory Show director Kyla McMillan said in a statement. ‚ÄúThis year, that comes through in the breadth of the gallery list, the expansion of Presents and Solo, and the work of our curators, whose sections trace histories of movement, exchange and cultural connection through the Caribbean. Rooted in New York, the fair brings together ambitious presentations that define the conversations of the year ahead, remaining in constant dialogue with the geographies and communities shaping contemporary art today.‚ÄĚ

	The full exhibitor list follows below.

	Galleries
